From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1905–1997 shipbuilding company in the United States

Bethlehem Shipbuilding Corporation

**Bethlehem Steel Corporation Shipbuilding Division** was created in 1905 when the [Bethlehem Steel](/wiki/Bethlehem_Steel "Bethlehem Steel") Corporation of [Bethlehem, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Bethlehem,_Pennsylvania "Bethlehem, Pennsylvania"), acquired the [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco")-based shipyard [Union Iron Works](/wiki/Union_Iron_Works "Union Iron Works").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Strohmeier_1963-2) In 1917, it was incorporated as **Bethlehem Shipbuilding Corporation, Limited**.

The division's headquarters were moved to [Quincy, Massachusetts](/wiki/Quincy,_Massachusetts "Quincy, Massachusetts"), after acquiring the [Fore River Shipyard](/wiki/Fore_River_Shipyard "Fore River Shipyard") in 1913.

In 1940, Bethlehem Shipbuilding was the largest of the "Big Three" U.S. shipbuilders that could build any ship,[[3]](#cite_note-3) followed by [Newport News Shipbuilding & Drydock](/wiki/Newport_News_Shipbuilding_%26_Drydock "Newport News Shipbuilding & Drydock") and [New York Shipbuilding](/wiki/New_York_Shipbuilding "New York Shipbuilding") Corporation (New York Ship). Bethlehem expanded shortly before and during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") as a result of the [Long Range Shipbuilding Program](/wiki/Long_Range_Shipbuilding_Program "Long Range Shipbuilding Program") and later the [Emergency Shipbuilding program](/wiki/Emergency_Shipbuilding_program "Emergency Shipbuilding program") orchestrated by the [United States Maritime Commission](/wiki/United_States_Maritime_Commission "United States Maritime Commission") and the [Two Ocean Navy](/wiki/Two-Ocean_Navy_Act "Two-Ocean Navy Act") program and its war-time successors by the military establishment.

In 1964, the now-corporate headquarters moved to [Sparrows Point, Maryland](/wiki/Sparrows_Point,_Maryland "Sparrows Point, Maryland"), southeast of [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), whose shipyard had been acquired in 1916.

The Quincy / Fore River yard was sold to [General Dynamics Corporation](/wiki/General_Dynamics_Corporation "General Dynamics Corporation") in the mid-1960s, and closed in 1986. The [Alameda Works Shipyard](/wiki/Alameda_Works_Shipyard "Alameda Works Shipyard") in [California](/wiki/California "California") was closed by Bethlehem Steel in the early 1970s, while the [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") facility (former [Union Iron Works](/wiki/Union_Iron_Works "Union Iron Works")) was sold to [British Aerospace](/wiki/British_Aerospace "British Aerospace") in the mid-1990s and survives today as [BAE Systems](/wiki/BAE_Systems "BAE Systems") San Francisco Ship Repair.

Bethlehem Steel ceased shipbuilding activities in 1997 in an attempt to preserve its core steelmaking operations.

The [Bethlehem Shipbuilding Corporation Hospital](/w/index.php?title=Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_Corporation_Hospital&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bethlehem Shipbuilding Corporation Hospital (page does not exist)"), first built to treat injured workers, was assigned to be on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_San_Francisco "National Register of Historic Places listings in San Francisco") in December 2022.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Shipyards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_Corporation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Shipyards")]

Shipyards owned or operated by Bethlehem:

### New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_Corporation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: New York")]

* Became part of Bethlehem with the purchase of United Shipyards on June 2, 1938 for $9,320,000[[6]](#cite_note-pmr_38_7_42-6)

* + [Bethlehem Mariners Harbor](/wiki/Bethlehem_Mariners_Harbor "Bethlehem Mariners Harbor"), [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"), New York (1938–1963[[7]](#cite_note-closed63-7)).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

* + [Bethlehem Brooklyn 56th Street](/wiki/Bethlehem_Brooklyn_56th_Street "Bethlehem Brooklyn 56th Street"), [Brooklyn, New York](/wiki/Brooklyn,_New_York "Brooklyn, New York") (1938–1963[[7]](#cite_note-closed63-7)).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

* + [Bethlehem Brooklyn 27th Street](/wiki/Bethlehem_Brooklyn_27th_Street "Bethlehem Brooklyn 27th Street") (1938–1963[[7]](#cite_note-closed63-7)).

* + [Hoboken Shipyard](/wiki/Hoboken_Shipyard "Hoboken Shipyard"), [Hoboken, New Jersey](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey") (1938–1984).[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

* + they were called the Staten Island Works, the Brooklyn 56th Street Works, the Brooklyn 27th Street Works and the Hoboken Works of the New York Plant of the Bethlehem Shipbuilding Corporation.[[13]](#cite_note-pmr_38_8_53-13)

* [Bethlehem Elizabethport](/wiki/Bethlehem_Elizabethport "Bethlehem Elizabethport"), [Elizabethport, New Jersey](/wiki/Elizabethport,_New_Jersey "Elizabethport, New Jersey") (1916–1921).[[14]](#cite_note-14)

* [Bayonne Naval Drydock](/wiki/Bayonne_Naval_Drydock "Bayonne Naval Drydock"), [Bayonne, New Jersey](/wiki/Bayonne,_New_Jersey "Bayonne, New Jersey"). Bethlehem used this drydock for ship repairs. Most workers were from Hoboken Shipyard.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Boston

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_Corporation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Boston")]

* [Fore River Shipyard](/wiki/Fore_River_Shipyard "Fore River Shipyard"), [Quincy, Massachusetts](/wiki/Quincy,_Massachusetts "Quincy, Massachusetts") (1913–1963[[7]](#cite_note-closed63-7)). Sold to [General Dynamics Corporation](/wiki/General_Dynamics_Corporation "General Dynamics Corporation").
* [Victory Plant Shipyard](/wiki/Victory_Plant_Shipyard "Victory Plant Shipyard"), [Quincy, Massachusetts](/wiki/Quincy,_Massachusetts "Quincy, Massachusetts") (1917–1919). The "Victory Yard" was constructed to build destroyers and free up the Fore River Yard for other vessels including the battlecruiser-turned-aircraft carrier [USS *Lexington* (CV-2)](/wiki/USS_Lexington_(CV-2) "USS Lexington (CV-2)").
* [Bethlehem Hingham Shipyard](/wiki/Bethlehem_Hingham_Shipyard "Bethlehem Hingham Shipyard"), [Hingham, Massachusetts](/wiki/Hingham,_Massachusetts "Hingham, Massachusetts") (1940–1945).[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* [Bethlehem Atlantic Works](/wiki/Bethlehem_Atlantic_Works "Bethlehem Atlantic Works"), [East Boston, Massachusetts](/wiki/East_Boston,_Massachusetts "East Boston, Massachusetts") (1853–1984).

### Baltimore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_Corporation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Baltimore")]

* [Bethlehem Sparrows Point Shipyard](/wiki/Bethlehem_Sparrows_Point_Shipyard "Bethlehem Sparrows Point Shipyard"), [Sparrows Point, Maryland](/wiki/Sparrows_Point,_Maryland "Sparrows Point, Maryland") (1914–1997).[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* [Bethlehem Fairfield Shipyard](/wiki/Bethlehem_Fairfield_Shipyard "Bethlehem Fairfield Shipyard"), [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), (1940–1945).[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* [Bethlehem Key Highway Shipyard](/wiki/Bethlehem_Key_Highway_Shipyard "Bethlehem Key Highway Shipyard"), Baltimore. The upper yard was sold to AME/Swirnow in 1983. The site now holds Ritz Carlton and [Harborview](/wiki/Harborview,_Baltimore "Harborview, Baltimore") communities next to [Baltimore Museum of Industry](/wiki/Baltimore_Museum_of_Industry "Baltimore Museum of Industry").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* [Bethlehem Fort McHenry Shipyard](/wiki/Bethlehem_Fort_McHenry_Shipyard "Bethlehem Fort McHenry Shipyard"), Baltimore. The lower yard on [Locust Point](/wiki/Locust_Point_Industrial_Area,_Baltimore "Locust Point Industrial Area, Baltimore") peninsula, it was sold to General Ship Repair in 1983. Now some [Port of Baltimore](/wiki/Port_of_Baltimore "Port of Baltimore") terminals.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### San Francisco

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_Corporation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: San Francisco")]

* [Union Iron Works](/wiki/Union_Iron_Works "Union Iron Works"), [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), California (1917–1981).
  + also called the Potrero Works and the Risdon Works of the Union plant of Bethlehem Steel
* [Alameda Works Shipyard](/wiki/Alameda_Works_Shipyard "Alameda Works Shipyard"), [Alameda, California](/wiki/Alameda,_California "Alameda, California") (1916–1956).
  + also called the Alameda Works of the Union plant of Bethlehem Steel
* [Hunters Point Drydocks](/wiki/Hunters_Point_Drydocks "Hunters Point Drydocks"), [Hunters Point, San Francisco, California](/wiki/Hunters_Point,_San_Francisco,_California "Hunters Point, San Francisco, California") (1908–1920). Acquired by the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy")

### Others

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_Corporation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Others")]

* [Bethlehem Shipbuilding San Pedro](/wiki/Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_San_Pedro "Bethlehem Shipbuilding San Pedro") on [Terminal Island](/wiki/Terminal_Island "Terminal Island"), formerly *Southwestern Shipbuilding*.
* [Bethlehem Steel Wilmington](/wiki/Bethlehem_Steel_Wilmington "Bethlehem Steel Wilmington") (aka [Harlan and Hollingsworth](/wiki/Harlan_and_Hollingsworth "Harlan and Hollingsworth")), [Wilmington, Delaware](/wiki/Wilmington,_Delaware "Wilmington, Delaware") (1904–1925, 1941–1945).[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* [Bethlehem Beaumont Shipyard](/wiki/Bethlehem_Beaumont_Shipyard "Bethlehem Beaumont Shipyard"), [Beaumont, Texas](/wiki/Beaumont,_Texas "Beaumont, Texas") (1948–1989). A major U.S. manufacturer of offshore drilling rigs, it produced 72.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* [Bethlehem Sabine](/wiki/Bethlehem_Sabine "Bethlehem Sabine"), [Port Arthur, Texas](/wiki/Port_Arthur,_Texas "Port Arthur, Texas"), (1985–1995). Sold to Texas Drydock Inc. in 1995.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
